_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against the 2018 NRS phenotype-based update, 2020 AAD Rosacea Care Guidelines, 2017 ROSCO consensus, and pivotal trial primary literature (ivermectin Stein 2014, brimonidine Fowler 2013, oxymetazoline Kircik 2018, minocycline 1.5% foam FX2016-11, doxycycline 40 mg modified-release Del Rosso 2007). Prevalence per Tan & Berg 2013 meta-analysis.

h1Title: Rosacea: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Rosacea is a chronic inflammatory facial skin disease characterized by recurrent flushing, persistent central-face erythema, telangiectasias, and inflammatory papules and pustules, driven by an overactive innate immune response and dysregulation of the cutaneous vasculature. It affects roughly 5% of adults worldwide and about 16 million adults in the United States, with peak onset between ages 30 and 50 and a strong predilection for fair-skinned individuals of Northern European descent. The disease centers on the cheeks, nose, chin, and forehead and runs a relapsing-remitting course shaped by identifiable triggers such as sun, heat, alcohol, and spicy food. Untreated, it can progress to phymatous skin thickening (most often rhinophyma) and ocular involvement that threatens vision. Targeted therapies including topical ivermectin, azelaic acid, brimonidine, oxymetazoline, and modified-release doxycycline now control symptoms in the majority of patients, with vascular laser and intense pulsed light addressing residual redness and visible vessels.

keyStats.prevalence: ~5.5% of adults globally (Tan & Berg 2013 meta-analysis); ~16 million US adults
keyStats.demographics: Women diagnosed 2-3x more often than men; men more likely to progress to phymatous disease
keyStats.avgAge: Onset typically age 30-50; rare before puberty
keyStats.globalCases: 415 million people worldwide (Global Burden of Skin Disease estimate)

definition: Rosacea (ICD-10: L71; L71.0 perioral dermatitis, L71.1 rhinophyma, L71.8 other rosacea, L71.9 rosacea unspecified) is a chronic, relapsing inflammatory disease of the central face driven by an exaggerated innate immune response, neurovascular dysregulation, and disordered cutaneous antimicrobial peptide processing. Cathelicidin LL-37 is overexpressed and abnormally cleaved by elevated kallikrein-5 in rosacea skin, producing the pro-inflammatory peptides that recruit neutrophils and drive vasodilation. The disease is now classified by phenotype rather than the older four-subtype system: the 2017 ROSCO global consensus and 2018 NRS update recommend identifying the diagnostic features (persistent central facial erythema and phymatous changes) and the major features (papules and pustules, telangiectasias, flushing, ocular manifestations) present in each individual patient. Severity ranges from mild transient flushing to disfiguring phymatous thickening of the nose (rhinophyma) and sight-threatening ocular rosacea with keratitis. Demodex folliculorum density is markedly increased in rosacea skin and contributes to inflammation, which is why anti-parasitic ivermectin is an effective treatment. Rosacea is managed primarily by dermatologists, with ophthalmology co-management when ocular involvement is present and ENT or plastic surgery involvement for advanced phymatous disease. The condition is lifelong but well controlled with modern therapy in most patients.

typesClassification.description: Persistent central facial erythema with episodic flushing and visible telangiectasias on the cheeks and nose. Skin is often sensitive, stinging easily with topical products. The 2018 NRS update folds this into phenotype-based assessment focused on erythema and flushing.

Inflammatory papules and small pustules on a background of central facial erythema, classically mistaken for acne. Comedones (blackheads or whiteheads) are absent, distinguishing it from acne vulgaris. Responds well to topical ivermectin, azelaic acid, and oral doxycycline.

Sebaceous gland hyperplasia and fibrosis producing thickened, irregular skin, most commonly on the nose (rhinophyma) but also chin (gnathophyma), forehead (metophyma), ears (otophyma), and eyelids (blepharophyma). Strong male predominance. Often coexists with the other phenotypes.

Affects roughly 50% of rosacea patients and can precede skin disease in 20% of cases. Presents with burning, foreign-body sensation, blepharitis, recurrent chalazia, meibomian gland dysfunction, and in advanced cases corneal neovascularization and ulceration that can threaten vision.

An uncommon variant marked by hard, yellow-brown or red papules and nodules on the cheeks and periorbital area, often without prominent background erythema. Biopsy shows non-caseating granulomas. Tends to be more resistant to standard therapy.

primarySymptoms: Recurrent flushing — episodic, transient redness of the face triggered by heat, alcohol, spicy food, sun, exercise, or strong emotion, lasting minutes to hours before fading and progressively contributing to fixed redness over years.
Persistent central facial erythema — fixed background redness across the cheeks, nose, chin, and central forehead that does not fade between flushing episodes and is the cardinal diagnostic feature.
Inflammatory papules and small dome-shaped pustules on a red background, distributed centrally on the face, lacking comedones (blackheads or whiteheads) — a key distinction from acne vulgaris.
Telangiectasias — fine, threadlike, visible blood vessels on the cheeks, nasal alae, and chin, most apparent in fair-skinned patients and easily seen in good lighting.
Skin sensitivity, stinging, and burning with topical products, sunscreen, soap, or temperature change; many patients have a long history of being unable to tolerate facial cosmetics.
Phymatous skin changes — thickened, irregular, bumpy skin from sebaceous gland hyperplasia and fibrosis, most often producing a bulbous nose (rhinophyma) and seen predominantly in men.
Ocular symptoms including burning, foreign-body sensation, dry gritty eyes, redness, photophobia, recurrent styes or chalazia, and crusting at the eyelid margins.
Edema of the central face, especially the forehead and cheeks, which can be intermittent or persistent in long-standing disease (Morbihan disease in severe cases).
Symmetric distribution centered on convex facial structures, with the eyelids, periocular skin, and perioral skin typically spared in classic disease.
Absence of comedones — unlike acne, blackheads and whiteheads are not part of rosacea, and their presence should prompt reconsideration of the diagnosis or concurrent acne.
earlyWarningSigns: Easy facial flushing in response to hot drinks, alcohol, spicy food, or temperature change that lasts longer than blushing should and recurs frequently in the 30s and 40s
Cheeks and nose that look pink even when calm and indoors, with stinging when sunscreen or cleanser is applied
A few persistent pink bumps on the cheeks or nose that come and go without forming blackheads
Visible thread-like vessels on the cheeks or nasal alae in good lighting, especially in fair-skinned individuals over 30
Eyes that feel gritty, burning, or watery without an obvious infection, with recurrent styes or chalazia
emergencySigns: Sudden severe eye pain with photophobia and decreased vision in a rosacea patient — possible rosacea keratitis or corneal ulcer, requiring same-day ophthalmology evaluation
Rapid central facial swelling, fever, and warmth — possible cellulitis or erysipelas superimposed on rosacea, requiring urgent assessment
New unilateral severe headache with facial flushing, palpitations, and sweating — flushing here is not rosacea but warrants workup for pheochromocytoma or carcinoid
Acute corneal opacity or visible vessels growing onto the cornea — sight-threatening neovascularization needing prompt ophthalmology referral

causes.cause: Innate immune dysregulation with abnormal cathelicidin processing
causes.description: Rosacea skin overexpresses toll-like receptor 2, kallikrein-5, and the antimicrobial peptide cathelicidin LL-37. Excess kallikrein-5 cleaves LL-37 into pro-inflammatory peptide fragments that recruit neutrophils, induce vasodilation, and drive the erythema, papules, and pustules. This pathway underlies the rationale for anti-inflammatory therapies such as low-dose doxycycline.

Cutaneous vessels in rosacea respond excessively to neuropeptides (substance P, CGRP, VIP) and to TRPV1/TRPV4 channel activation by heat, capsaicin, and ultraviolet radiation. Repeated vasodilation over years leads to fixed telangiectasias and persistent erythema. This explains why heat, sun, exercise, alcohol, and spicy food are universal triggers.

Demodex mite counts in facial follicles are 5-10 times higher in rosacea patients than in healthy controls. Mite proteins and Bacillus oleronius released from dying mites trigger an inflammatory immune response. Anti-parasitic ivermectin reduces mite burden and is one of the most effective topical treatments.

UV exposure damages dermal collagen and elastin, weakens vessel-wall support, generates reactive oxygen species, and upregulates matrix metalloproteinases. Daily broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen reduces flare frequency and slows long-term progression.

Twin studies estimate heritability near 46%. Genome-wide association studies identify HLA-DRB1*03:01, HLA-DQB1*02:01, and HLA-DQA1*05:01 as risk alleles. A family history of rosacea more than doubles personal risk.

Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth and Helicobacter pylori carriage are more prevalent in rosacea patients than in controls, and eradication studies show variable improvement in papulopustular disease. The cutaneous microbiome composition also differs from unaffected skin.

riskFactors.factor: Fair skin and Northern European ancestry
riskFactors.description: Highest prevalence among Fitzpatrick skin types I-II of Northern and Western European descent, reaching 10% in some Scandinavian and Irish cohorts. Less commonly diagnosed in darker skin types, where erythema is harder to detect and the disease is frequently underrecognized.
Onset before puberty is rare; new-onset rosacea typically develops in the third to fifth decade. Disease tends to stabilize or progress slowly with age.

riskFactors.factor: Female sex (for erythema and papulopustular subtypes)
riskFactors.description: Women are diagnosed 2-3 times more often than men for the erythematotelangiectatic and papulopustular phenotypes, although men predominate in phymatous disease.
Heritability estimated at 46% in twin studies. A first-degree relative with rosacea more than doubles personal risk, particularly for early-onset disease.

Long-term sun exposure damages vessel-wall support and drives matrix metalloproteinase activation, both implicated in fixed erythema and telangiectasia. Daily SPF 30+ measurably reduces flare frequency.

riskFactors.factor: Frequent flushing triggers (alcohol, spicy food, hot drinks, heat, exercise, stress)
riskFactors.description: Repeated vasodilation episodes amplify neurovascular dysregulation and contribute to fixed erythema over years. Trigger avoidance reduces flush frequency by 30-50% in most patients.

riskFactors.factor: Topical corticosteroid use on the face
riskFactors.description: Mid- and high-potency facial steroids produce a steroid-induced rosacea-like eruption with rebound erythema and papulopustules on withdrawal. Discontinuation under specialist guidance is the first step in treatment.

riskFactors.factor: Helicobacter pylori infection and small intestinal bacterial overgrowth
riskFactors.description: Both conditions are more prevalent in rosacea patients; eradication has shown variable benefit in papulopustular disease, particularly in patients with concurrent dyspepsia.
Smoking is associated with a modest increase in rosacea risk and worsens phymatous disease through chronic oxidative stress and impaired dermal repair.

riskFactors.factor: Migraine and inflammatory bowel disease comorbidity
riskFactors.description: Both share neurovascular and innate-immune dysregulation pathways with rosacea. Migraine prevalence is roughly 1.5x higher in rosacea patients; ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease are also enriched in this group.

affectedDemographics: US prevalence: ~5.5% of adults (~16 million people), per Tan & Berg 2013 meta-analysis
Northern European countries report the highest rates, with Swedish, Estonian, and Irish cohorts reaching 10-22% in office-worker populations
Underdiagnosed in skin of color: dermatoscopic and biopsy series suggest true prevalence in Black and South Asian patients is closer to 4-5%, not the historically reported <1%
Women are diagnosed 2-3 times more often than men, but men more often develop phymatous disease (rhinophyma)
Ocular involvement affects approximately 50% of rosacea patients and precedes skin disease in 20% of cases
diagnosisOverview: Rosacea is a clinical diagnosis. The 2017 ROSCO global consensus and the 2018 National Rosacea Society phenotype-based update define the diagnosis by the presence of either of two diagnostic features — persistent central facial erythema or phymatous skin changes — or by two or more major features (papules and pustules, telangiectasias, flushing, ocular manifestations). A focused history identifies typical triggers, family history, prior use of topical corticosteroids, and ocular symptoms that patients often do not connect to their skin disease. Examination is conducted in good natural light and includes the cheeks, nose, chin, central forehead, eyelid margins, and the conjunctiva. Comedones, if present, exclude pure rosacea and indicate acne or an overlap. Skin biopsy is unnecessary in typical disease but useful to rule out lupus erythematosus, sarcoidosis, granulomatous rosacea, or demodicosis when presentation is atypical. Dermoscopy reveals dilated polygonal vessels and demodex tails in characteristic patterns. Ocular rosacea is diagnosed clinically with slit-lamp examination by an ophthalmologist when patients report burning, photophobia, recurrent styes, or visible eyelid margin inflammation; tear breakup time and meibomian gland imaging quantify dysfunction. Differential diagnosis includes acne vulgaris (comedones present), seborrheic dermatitis (greasy yellow scale, nasolabial fold predominance), perioral dermatitis (perioral and periorbital papules sparing the vermilion border), systemic lupus erythematosus (malar rash, photosensitivity, systemic symptoms, positive ANA), and carcinoid or pheochromocytoma in patients with severe flushing accompanied by palpitations, diarrhea, or hypertension. Standard photography under reproducible lighting at diagnosis aids monitoring and treatment response, since erythema and telangiectasia can be hard to track subjectively over months.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination by a dermatologist using the 2017 ROSCO phenotype framework
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms diagnosis based on diagnostic features (persistent central erythema or phymatous changes) or two or more major features. No imaging or laboratory test is required for typical disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Visual inspection of the central face, eyelid margins, and the rest of the face in good light. The clinician will ask about triggers and review current skincare. The visit takes 15-20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides an objective baseline to track erythema, papule and pustule counts, and telangiectasia over treatment. Particularly useful for laser and biologic therapy decisions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A short series of standardized photos in fixed lighting, often with cross-polarized images that highlight redness. Painless; adds 5-10 minutes to the visit.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms ocular rosacea, grades meibomian gland dysfunction, and detects corneal involvement. Recommended at diagnosis when ocular symptoms are present and yearly thereafter in active ocular disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed by an ophthalmologist or optometrist. Eyes are examined under magnification with bright light; fluorescein dye may be applied to highlight corneal surface changes. The exam is painless and takes 10-15 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for atypical presentations to rule out lupus erythematosus, sarcoidosis, granulomatous rosacea, demodicosis, or cutaneous lymphoma. Histology in classic rosacea shows perivascular and perifollicular lymphohistiocytic infiltrate, dilated vessels, and solar elastosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 3-4 mm circular skin sample taken under local anesthesia. One or two sutures, mild soreness, healing over 1-2 weeks. Results typically return in 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive visualization of vascular patterns, demodex tails (white scaly follicular plugs), and follicular features. Useful in atypical or skin-of-color presentations where surface redness is harder to see.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld dermatoscope is placed against the skin with gel or contact medium for short periods. Painless; takes a few minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Demodex density assessment (standardized skin surface biopsy)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies follicular Demodex burden when demodicosis or a refractory papulopustular eruption is suspected. Counts above 5 mites per square centimeter are considered pathologically increased.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A drop of cyanoacrylate glue is placed on a glass slide pressed onto facial skin for one minute; the sample is examined under a microscope. Painless and outpatient.

diagnosticTests.purpose: When flushing is severe, sustained, accompanied by palpitations, diarrhea, sweating, or hypertension, or refractory to standard rosacea therapy, rule out carcinoid syndrome (24-hour urinary 5-HIAA), pheochromocytoma (plasma free metanephrines), mastocytosis (serum tryptase), and medication-induced flushing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Specific blood and urine tests ordered by the dermatologist or referring physician; arranged with the patient's primary care team.

treatmentOverview: Rosacea treatment matches the patient's dominant phenotype rather than an outdated subtype label, and the 2020 American Academy of Dermatology Rosacea Care Guidelines structure recommendations around the visible features present. Background care for every patient includes daily broad-spectrum mineral sunscreen SPF 30 or higher, a gentle non-soap cleanser, and identification of personal triggers. Persistent erythema responds best to topical alpha-adrenergic agonists — brimonidine 0.33% gel once daily produces visible erythema reduction within 30 minutes that lasts about 12 hours, and oxymetazoline 1% cream achieves comparable results with a lower risk of rebound erythema. Telangiectasias and fixed background redness are addressed with vascular laser (pulsed-dye laser at 595 nm) or intense pulsed light, with 60-80% improvement after 3-4 sessions spaced 4-6 weeks apart. For papules and pustules, topical ivermectin 1% cream once daily is now first-line based on pivotal Stein 2014 trials showing higher clear or almost-clear rates than metronidazole at 16 weeks. Azelaic acid 15% gel and metronidazole 0.75-1% gel or cream remain effective alternatives. Modified-release doxycycline 40 mg once daily (sub-antimicrobial anti-inflammatory dosing per Del Rosso 2007) is the preferred oral therapy for moderate-to-severe papulopustular disease, with efficacy similar to 100 mg doxycycline but a markedly lower side-effect and antibiotic-resistance profile. Minocycline 1.5% foam (FMX103, FOAMs trials 2019) offers an effective topical alternative for patients who prefer to avoid oral antibiotics. Severe refractory papulopustular and phymatous disease may require isotretinoin 0.25-0.5 mg/kg/day, which reduces sebaceous activity and fibrosis. Established phymatous changes are treated surgically — electrosurgery, dermabrasion, CO2 laser ablation, or cold-steel paring — with excellent cosmetic outcomes when performed by an experienced operator. Ocular rosacea is co-managed with ophthalmology; treatment includes warm compresses, lid hygiene, artificial tears, topical cyclosporine 0.05%, and oral doxycycline for moderate-to-severe disease. Trigger avoidance, daily sunscreen, and consistent skincare reduce flare frequency by 30-50% across all phenotypes.

medicalTreatments.name: Ivermectin 1% cream (Soolantra) applied once daily at bedtime
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-parasitic and anti-inflammatory topical that reduces Demodex burden and downregulates skin inflammation. First-line for papulopustular rosacea per the 2020 AAD guidelines, with pivotal evidence from the Stein 2014 phase 3 trials showing superiority over vehicle and a head-to-head advantage over metronidazole at 16 weeks. Pregnancy category B.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Investigator Global Assessment success (clear or almost clear) in 38-40% at 12 weeks versus 12% for vehicle; reduces inflammatory lesion counts by 76% at 16 weeks in head-to-head comparison with metronidazole.

medicalTreatments.name: Azelaic acid 15% gel or foam applied twice daily
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-inflammatory dicarboxylic acid that inhibits kallikrein-5 and reduces cathelicidin-driven inflammation. Effective for papulopustular and mild erythematous disease. Mild stinging on application is the most common side effect; pregnancy category B.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Investigator Global Assessment success in approximately 40% at 12 weeks; reduces papule and pustule counts by 50-60% in randomized trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Metronidazole 0.75% gel or 1% cream applied once or twice daily
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-inflammatory topical with decades of clinical use. Useful as maintenance therapy after a course of oral doxycycline or ivermectin, or as first-line for milder papulopustular disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces inflammatory lesion counts by approximately 50% versus vehicle; somewhat less effective than ivermectin 1% in head-to-head comparison.

medicalTreatments.name: Brimonidine 0.33% gel (Mirvaso) applied once daily in the morning
medicalTreatments.description: Selective alpha-2 adrenergic agonist that produces rapid vasoconstriction of cutaneous vessels and visible reduction of persistent erythema within 30 minutes, lasting about 12 hours. Pivotal Fowler 2013 trials demonstrated efficacy; rebound erythema can occur in 10-20% of users and patients should be counseled to start a few times per week before daily use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Two-grade improvement on Clinician and Patient Erythema Assessment scales in 25-30% at 12 hours after application; effect persists with continued use.

medicalTreatments.name: Oxymetazoline 1% cream (Rhofade) applied once daily in the morning
medicalTreatments.description: Alpha-1A adrenergic agonist with a similar erythema-reducing effect to brimonidine but a lower reported rate of rebound erythema in pivotal Kircik 2018 trials. Useful for patients who experience rebound on brimonidine.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Two-grade improvement on Clinician and Subject Erythema Assessment scales in 12-15% at 3 hours and 18% at 6 hours after application; durable through week 29 in extension studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline 40 mg modified-release once daily (sub-antimicrobial dose, Oracea)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-inflammatory rather than antibacterial dosing — too low to select for bacterial resistance but sufficient to inhibit matrix metalloproteinases and neutrophil chemotaxis. Pivotal Del Rosso 2007 trials demonstrated efficacy equivalent to 100 mg doxycycline with markedly fewer gastrointestinal side effects. Preferred oral agent per AAD 2020.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces inflammatory lesion counts by 60-65% at 16 weeks; non-inferior to standard 100 mg doxycycline with a cleaner safety profile.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline 100 mg once or twice daily (standard antimicrobial dose)
medicalTreatments.description: Higher-dose antibiotic regimen used for ocular rosacea, severe papulopustular disease, and during initial control phases. Tapered to modified-release 40 mg or topical maintenance after 8-12 weeks to limit antibiotic exposure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves clear or near-clear papulopustular disease in 60-70% of patients at 12 weeks; effective for moderate-to-severe ocular rosacea.

medicalTreatments.name: Minocycline 1.5% foam (Zilxi/FMX103) applied once daily
medicalTreatments.description: Topical tetracycline-class anti-inflammatory developed to avoid systemic antibiotic exposure. Approved 2020 for moderate-to-severe papulopustular rosacea on the basis of the FX2016-11 and FX2016-12 pivotal trials.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IGA success in approximately 50% at week 12; reduces inflammatory lesion counts by 60-70% versus vehicle.

medicalTreatments.name: Isotretinoin 0.25-0.5 mg/kg/day for 3-6 months
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severe, refractory papulopustular rosacea and early phymatous disease. Reduces sebaceous gland activity, dermal fibrosis, and inflammation. Requires pregnancy prevention program (iPLEDGE in US), monthly lab monitoring, and counseling on mood, lipids, and liver enzymes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clear or almost-clear papulopustular disease in 75-90% at 6 months; modest improvement in early phymatous changes.

medicalTreatments.name: Pulsed-dye laser (PDL, 595 nm) or intense pulsed light (IPL), 3-4 sessions every 4-6 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Selective vascular photothermolysis that targets hemoglobin in dilated dermal vessels, reducing telangiectasias and background erythema. Mainstay for vascular features that do not fully respond to topical or oral therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 60-80% reduction in telangiectasias and persistent erythema after 3-4 sessions, with durable results over years when sun protection is maintained.

surgicalOptions.name: Rhinophyma reduction with electrosurgery, dermabrasion, CO2 laser, or cold-steel paring
surgicalOptions.description: Mainstay for established rhinophyma. The hypertrophic sebaceous tissue is shaved or ablated layer by layer down to a contoured shape that approximates the original anatomy, leaving the deep dermis intact for re-epithelialization. Performed under local or general anesthesia depending on extent.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Excellent cosmetic outcome in 85-95% of patients in experienced hands; recurrence is uncommon when isotretinoin and trigger control are continued.

surgicalOptions.name: Excision of granulomatous nodules or Morbihan disease
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical debulking of persistent firm nodules and lymphedematous tissue in refractory granulomatous rosacea or Morbihan disease, often combined with isotretinoin and intralesional corticosteroids.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 70-85% of selected patients; recurrence is reduced by ongoing systemic therapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Niacinamide 4-5% topical serum
alternativeTreatments.description: Anti-inflammatory vitamin B3 derivative with randomized evidence supporting reduced erythema and improved skin barrier function in rosacea. Reasonable adjunct to first-line therapy; well-tolerated and non-prescription.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sulfacetamide 10% / sulfur 5% cleanser or lotion
alternativeTreatments.description: Antimicrobial and keratolytic combination with decades of clinical use for papulopustular rosacea. Useful for patients who tolerate sulfur and want a non-antibiotic option; effect size is smaller than ivermectin or azelaic acid.

alternativeTreatments.name: Green tea polyphenol topical 2%
alternativeTreatments.description: Anti-inflammatory and antioxidant adjunct with small-trial evidence of reduced erythema and papule counts in rosacea. Reasonable maintenance addition; not a substitute for guideline-directed therapy.

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to a dermatologist when over-the-counter measures and a gentle skincare routine fail to control facial redness, when papules and pustules persist for more than 4-6 weeks, when telangiectasias or phymatous changes appear, when topical corticosteroids have been applied to the face and triggered a flare, and when ocular symptoms suggest ocular rosacea. Early specialist care reduces long-term progression to fixed redness, telangiectasia, and phymatous disease.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a board-certified dermatologist with experience in vascular laser and intense pulsed light therapy, as these modalities meaningfully improve outcomes for telangiectasia and persistent erythema. For ocular involvement, a dermatologist who co-manages with an ophthalmologist (or a dedicated cornea specialist) is ideal. For phymatous disease, look for a clinician experienced in CO2 laser, dermabrasion, or electrosurgical contouring — these are technique-dependent procedures, and operator experience drives the cosmetic result.
hospitalCriteria: Dermatology service with vascular laser and intense pulsed light capability
Co-management with ophthalmology for moderate-to-severe ocular rosacea
Access to isotretinoin prescribing under iPLEDGE (US) or equivalent pregnancy-prevention programs
Surgical capability for rhinophyma reduction (CO2 laser, electrosurgery, or dermabrasion)
Standardized photographic documentation for monitoring treatment response over months
preventionStrategies: Apply broad-spectrum mineral sunscreen SPF 30 or higher every morning regardless of weather — daily UV protection reduces flare frequency and slows progression to telangiectasia and fixed erythema
Identify and avoid personal flushing triggers using a 2-3 week diary covering hot drinks, alcohol, spicy food, exercise, heat, stress, and topical products
Use only fragrance-free, soap-free gentle cleansers and avoid abrasive scrubs, alcohol-based toners, and retinoids during active flares
Switch facial topical corticosteroids to non-steroid anti-inflammatories under specialist guidance — long-term facial steroid use causes steroid-induced rosacea that is harder to treat
Treat ocular surface disease early with lid hygiene and artificial tears to prevent progression to corneal involvement
Stop smoking and limit alcohol — both contribute to vessel-wall damage and disease progression over years
lifestyleModifications: Wash the face twice daily with a non-foaming, fragrance-free cleanser using lukewarm (not hot) water and pat dry without rubbing
Apply a moisturizer formulated for sensitive or rosacea-prone skin within 3 minutes of washing to support the skin barrier
Layer mineral sunscreen (zinc oxide or titanium dioxide) over moisturizer each morning; reapply every 2 hours during prolonged sun exposure
Choose green-tinted color-correcting primers or foundations to camouflage erythema; avoid heavy long-wear formulations that require harsh removers
Avoid hot showers, saunas, and prolonged exposure to direct heat sources; cool the face with a damp cloth after exercise
Sip cold or room-temperature drinks instead of hot beverages during flare-prone periods, and use a straw if possible
Manage stress with structured techniques (mindfulness, paced breathing, cognitive behavioral therapy) — stress is a documented trigger in 60-80% of patients
Maintain lid hygiene with warm compresses and gentle eyelid cleansing once or twice daily if you have ocular symptoms

recommended: Mediterranean-style eating pattern with vegetables, fruits, whole grains, legumes, olive oil, and fatty fish — observational data link adherence to lower rosacea severity
Omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish or supplements 1-2 g/day — small randomized data support reduced ocular rosacea symptoms
Water and room-temperature non-caffeinated drinks to maintain hydration without triggering flushing
Prebiotic and probiotic foods (yogurt, kefir, fermented vegetables) — emerging data support a gut-skin axis in rosacea
Foods low on the personal trigger list, as identified through a structured food diary
avoid: Hot drinks (coffee, tea, soup) — heat is a more reliable trigger than caffeine; cold versions of the same drinks are often well-tolerated
Alcohol, particularly red wine and spirits — well-documented vasodilator; beer is generally less triggering
Spicy foods rich in capsaicin (chili peppers, hot sauces) — activate TRPV1 channels and provoke flushing within minutes
Histamine-rich foods (aged cheese, cured meat, certain fish) in patients who report flushing after these meals
Sugary and ultra-processed foods, which are associated with systemic inflammation

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate-intensity exercise is encouraged for cardiovascular and metabolic health, and rosacea is not a contraindication. Reduce flushing during workouts by exercising in cool environments, using a fan, sipping cold water, and cooling the face with a damp cloth between sets. Brief shorter sessions tolerated better than prolonged sustained heat exposure for many patients. Hot yoga and saunas reliably trigger flares and are best avoided during active disease.
dailyManagement: Apply prescribed topical therapy at the same time each day — ivermectin or azelaic acid at bedtime, brimonidine or oxymetazoline in the morning if used
Use mineral sunscreen SPF 30+ every morning, even on cloudy days and indoors near windows
Keep a brief trigger diary for the first 8-12 weeks to identify personal flushing patterns
Carry a small cool-water mister or facial wipe for quick cooling after meals, exercise, or heat exposure
Schedule yearly dermatology follow-up to reassess phenotype and treatment, and ophthalmology review if ocular features are present
prognosis: Rosacea is a chronic relapsing disease that cannot be cured permanently, but most patients reach excellent control with phenotype-matched therapy and trigger management. Topical ivermectin and azelaic acid achieve clear or near-clear papulopustular disease in 40-55% by 16 weeks, and oral modified-release doxycycline lifts response rates to 60-70%. Vascular laser and IPL deliver 60-80% reduction in telangiectasia and fixed erythema after 3-4 sessions, with results lasting years under consistent sun protection. Without treatment, the typical course is gradual progression over a decade or more from intermittent flushing to fixed erythema, telangiectasia, and in a subset of patients (predominantly men) phymatous changes. Ocular rosacea, if untreated, can progress to corneal neovascularization, scarring, and vision loss in a small but important minority. The decisive prognostic factor is consistent daily sun protection plus appropriate phenotype-directed therapy maintained over years; with both in place, most patients live with minimal day-to-day impact and avoid the disfiguring complications.
recoveryTimeline: Topical therapy for papulopustular rosacea (ivermectin, azelaic acid, metronidazole, minocycline foam) typically shows meaningful improvement by week 4 and peak effect by week 12-16. Oral modified-release doxycycline reduces papule and pustule counts within 2-4 weeks and reaches maximum effect by 12 weeks. Brimonidine and oxymetazoline reduce erythema within 30 minutes to 3 hours of application, with the effect lasting 8-12 hours. Vascular laser and IPL produce visible vessel and erythema reduction within 1-2 weeks of each session; full effect builds over 3-4 sessions across 3-6 months. Rhinophyma reduction shows the cosmetic result immediately after re-epithelialization at 2-4 weeks.
complications: Rhinophyma and other phymatous changes — irreversible thickening and disfigurement of the nose, chin, ears, or eyelids that requires surgical reduction once established
Ocular rosacea progressing to corneal neovascularization, scarring, and vision loss — affects about 50% of rosacea patients in milder forms; sight-threatening in roughly 5%
Persistent telangiectasia and fixed erythema that does not fully fade between flares and only partially responds to topicals — addressed with vascular laser or IPL
Steroid-induced rosacea and rebound erythema — from inappropriate use of mid- or high-potency facial topical corticosteroids, often presenting with a sudden worsening on withdrawal

Psychological and quality-of-life impact — rosacea patients show elevated rates of anxiety, depression, and social avoidance, with measurable improvements once erythema and papulopustules are controlled
Morbihan disease (solid persistent facial edema) — rare but disfiguring chronic lymphedema of the upper face, requiring long-term combined medical and surgical management

supportResources.name: National Rosacea Society
supportResources.description: Leading US patient organization with trigger trackers, treatment guides, research updates, and a survey-based educational network.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Rosacea
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of diagnosis, treatment, and lifestyle care from the US dermatology specialty society.

supportResources.name: British Association of Dermatologists — Rosacea Patient Information Leaflet
supportResources.description: Evidence-based UK patient leaflet aligned with NHS treatment pathways.

supportResources.name: DermNet — Rosacea
supportResources.description: Comprehensive image-rich educational resource on rosacea phenotypes, treatments, and differential diagnosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acne Vulgaris
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acne shows comedones (blackheads and whiteheads) along with papules and pustules and tends to involve the forehead, jawline, chest, and back. Rosacea does not produce comedones, centers on the cheeks and nose, includes persistent erythema and flushing, and starts after age 30 in most cases. The two can coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Seborrheic dermatitis produces greasy yellowish scale in the nasolabial folds, eyebrows, scalp, and central chest, often without papules or pustules. Rosacea centers on the cheeks and nose with persistent erythema and inflammatory papules but lacks the greasy scale. The two often coexist and the overlap is sometimes called rosacea-seborrhea.

confusedWithConditions.name: Perioral Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Perioral dermatitis produces small clustered papules and micropustules around the mouth, nostrils, and eyes with a clear zone immediately adjacent to the vermilion border. Rosacea favors the cheeks and nose, includes prominent flushing and telangiectasia, and lacks the characteristic perioral sparing. Both can be triggered or worsened by topical corticosteroids.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lupus (Systemic Lupus Erythematosus)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: The lupus malar rash crosses the bridge of the nose and spares the nasolabial folds, is typically smooth without papules or telangiectasias, worsens sharply with sun, and is accompanied by systemic features (joint pain, fatigue, mouth ulcers) and positive ANA serology. Rosacea includes papules and pustules, telangiectasias, and lacks systemic involvement.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis (Facial Variant)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Facial psoriasis produces well-demarcated plaques with silvery-white scale, often on the hairline, eyebrows, and ears, and is usually part of a wider pattern of disease on elbows, knees, scalp, or nails. Rosacea has no thick silvery scale, centers on the central face, and includes flushing and telangiectasias.

relatedConditions.name: Acne Vulgaris
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common differential diagnosis for papulopustular rosacea and a condition that can coexist on the same face.

relatedConditions.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently coexists with rosacea, particularly in the nasolabial folds and central face; the overlap is termed rosacea-seborrhea.

relatedConditions.name: Perioral Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares a steroid-induced trigger and overlapping treatment with rosacea but spares the cheeks and central face.

relatedConditions.name: Rhinophyma
relatedConditions.relevance: Phymatous endpoint of long-standing rosacea, most often affecting men, addressed surgically.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another chronic immune-mediated facial differential; both are managed in dermatology and can rarely overlap.

faqs.question: What does rosacea look like?
faqs.answer: Rosacea presents as persistent redness across the cheeks, nose, chin, and central forehead, often with thread-like visible blood vessels and small red bumps or pus-filled pimples. Many patients also experience episodes of intense flushing triggered by heat, sun, alcohol, or spicy food. Unlike acne, there are no blackheads or whiteheads.

faqs.question: Is rosacea curable?
faqs.answer: Rosacea is not curable, but it is highly controllable. With phenotype-matched therapy — topical ivermectin or azelaic acid, oral modified-release doxycycline, brimonidine or oxymetazoline for redness, and vascular laser for visible vessels — most patients reach clear or near-clear skin and prevent progression. Treatment is usually lifelong with periodic maintenance.

faqs.question: What triggers a rosacea flare?
faqs.answer: Common triggers include sun exposure, hot drinks, alcohol (especially red wine), spicy food, hot weather, vigorous exercise, emotional stress, harsh skincare products, and topical corticosteroids applied to the face. Each patient typically has a personal short list identified through a 2-3 week trigger diary. Avoidance reduces flare frequency by 30-50% in most patients.

faqs.question: What is the difference between rosacea and acne?
faqs.answer: Acne shows comedones (blackheads and whiteheads) along with papules and pustules and tends to involve the forehead, jawline, chest, and back. Rosacea lacks comedones, centers on the cheeks and nose, includes persistent redness and flushing, and typically starts after age 30. The two can coexist and may need different treatments.

faqs.question: Can rosacea affect the eyes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ocular rosacea affects about 50% of rosacea patients and can precede skin disease in 20%. Symptoms include burning, gritty foreign-body sensation, watery or dry eyes, redness, photophobia, and recurrent styes or chalazia. Untreated ocular rosacea can progress to corneal damage and vision loss, so prompt ophthalmology evaluation is important.

faqs.question: What is rhinophyma?
faqs.answer: Rhinophyma is a thickening and disfigurement of the nose caused by long-standing rosacea, marked by enlarged sebaceous glands and fibrosis. It produces a bulbous, irregular, often reddened nose and predominantly affects men. Treatment is surgical — electrosurgery, CO2 laser, dermabrasion, or cold-steel paring — with excellent cosmetic outcomes in experienced hands.

faqs.question: Is ivermectin cream effective for rosacea?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ivermectin 1% cream (Soolantra) is now first-line for papulopustular rosacea per the 2020 American Academy of Dermatology guidelines. The pivotal Stein 2014 trials showed clear or almost-clear skin in 38-40% of patients at 12 weeks and superior performance to metronidazole at 16 weeks. It works by reducing Demodex mite burden and skin inflammation.

faqs.question: Does brimonidine gel help facial redness?
faqs.answer: Brimonidine 0.33% gel is a selective alpha-2 adrenergic agonist that visibly reduces persistent redness within 30 minutes of application and lasts about 12 hours. It does not treat papules or pustules. Rebound erythema affects 10-20% of users; starting with intermittent use and titrating up reduces this risk.

faqs.question: Why is low-dose doxycycline used for rosacea?
faqs.answer: Modified-release doxycycline 40 mg once daily (Oracea) delivers an anti-inflammatory dose too low to select for bacterial resistance. The Del Rosso 2007 pivotal trials showed efficacy comparable to 100 mg doxycycline with far fewer side effects and no antibiotic-resistance pressure. It is the preferred oral therapy for moderate-to-severe papulopustular rosacea.

faqs.question: Can laser treatment help rosacea?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pulsed-dye laser (595 nm) and intense pulsed light reduce telangiectasias and persistent redness by 60-80% after 3-4 sessions spaced 4-6 weeks apart. Results last years with daily sun protection. Laser does not treat active papules or pustules, which need topical or oral therapy. Most plans complement laser with maintenance topical therapy.

faqs.question: Is rosacea hereditary?
faqs.answer: Rosacea has a heritability of around 46% from twin studies, with risk genes mapped to HLA-DRB1, HLA-DQB1, and HLA-DQA1 loci. A first-degree relative with rosacea more than doubles personal risk. Lifestyle and environmental triggers determine when and how severely the disease appears.

faqs.question: Can rosacea occur in skin of color?
faqs.answer: Yes, although it is frequently underdiagnosed in Black, South Asian, and other darker skin types because redness is harder to detect against more deeply pigmented skin. Dermoscopy, careful examination in good light, and attention to papules, pustules, telangiectasias, and flushing improve recognition. True prevalence is closer to 4-5% in skin of color, not the historically reported under 1%.

faqs.question: Should I use steroids on rosacea?
faqs.answer: Topical corticosteroids should not be used long-term on rosacea. Mid- and high-potency facial steroids cause steroid-induced rosacea with rebound redness and pustules on withdrawal. Short courses under specialist guidance are sometimes used for severe inflammation, but maintenance therapy uses non-steroid agents like ivermectin, azelaic acid, or metronidazole.

faqs.question: What is the best sunscreen for rosacea?
faqs.answer: Mineral sunscreens containing zinc oxide and titanium dioxide are best tolerated because they sit on the skin surface rather than absorbing UV through chemical reactions. Look for fragrance-free, alcohol-free SPF 30 or higher, ideally tinted to neutralize redness. Daily use measurably reduces flare frequency and slows long-term progression.

faqs.question: Can rosacea be prevented?
faqs.answer: Rosacea cannot be prevented from developing in genetically susceptible people, but progression can be slowed. Daily sunscreen, identifying and avoiding personal triggers, gentle skincare, avoiding facial corticosteroids, and early dermatology treatment of mild redness or bumps all reduce flare frequency and limit progression to telangiectasia and phymatous disease.

faqs.question: Does diet affect rosacea?
faqs.answer: Diet is not a primary cause, but specific foods reliably trigger flushing in many patients — hot drinks, alcohol (especially red wine), spicy foods rich in capsaicin, and histamine-rich foods. A Mediterranean-style eating pattern and adequate omega-3 intake are associated with milder disease. A short food diary identifies personal triggers.

faqs.question: How long does rosacea treatment take to work?
faqs.answer: Topical therapy improves papules and pustules noticeably by week 4 and peaks at 12-16 weeks. Oral modified-release doxycycline reduces lesion counts within 2-4 weeks. Brimonidine and oxymetazoline reduce redness within 30 minutes to 3 hours of application. Vascular laser delivers visible improvement after each session, with full effect across 3-4 treatments.

faqs.question: Is rosacea linked to mental health problems?
faqs.answer: Yes. Anxiety, depression, and social avoidance are significantly more common in rosacea patients than in the general population, particularly in those with prominent facial redness or phymatous changes. Effective skin treatment measurably improves quality of life and mood; integrated mental-health support is appropriate when symptoms are significant.

faqs.question: Can children get rosacea?
faqs.answer: Childhood rosacea is rare. New facial flushing, papules, or pustules in children should prompt evaluation for other causes including atopic dermatitis, perioral dermatitis, juvenile dermatomyositis, lupus, and demodicosis. When pediatric rosacea is diagnosed, ocular involvement is often a leading feature and warrants ophthalmology co-management.

faqs.question: Is rosacea related to Helicobacter pylori?
faqs.answer: There is a modest association between Helicobacter pylori carriage and rosacea, particularly papulopustular disease in patients with dyspepsia. Eradication has produced variable improvement in trials. Testing and treatment are reasonable when gastrointestinal symptoms coexist, but H. pylori is not considered a primary cause.

faqs.question: Does makeup make rosacea worse?
faqs.answer: Most non-irritating mineral-based makeup is well tolerated and can camouflage redness effectively. Green-tinted primers and yellow-toned foundations neutralize background erythema. Avoid heavy long-wear formulations that need harsh removers, alcohol-based products, and fragranced cosmetics. Removing makeup gently with a fragrance-free cleanser at the end of each day reduces irritation.

simpleName: Rosacea (chronic facial redness with papules and visible vessels)
metaDescription: Rosacea: persistent facial redness, flushing, bumps and visible vessels. Symptoms, triggers, ivermectin, laser, and eye care reviewed by our medical board.
